events.title: Audiotonix - Soundtech Group - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by a division of the Commission constituted by a direction issued pursuant to section 19 of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Audiotonix Limited (Audiotonix)’s proposed acquisition, via Sound
Bidco S.r.l. and Sound Bidco (Denmark) ApS, of 100% of the shares
in Soundtech Group S.r.l. (Soundtech Group) (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Audiotonix, is a wholly-owned subsidiary of Audiotonix
Group Limited (Audiotonix Group), an UK-headquartered group
active in the design, engineering and manufacturing of professional
audio mixing consoles, associated equipment such as networking
(allowing consoles to connect with other consoles), interface
products (used to physically control music editing and mixing
remotely from a console), software (e.g., audio processing, control
software and operating systems) and spare parts. Audiotonix Group
is ultimately owned by PAI Partners S.à r.l. (PAI Partners), an
independent private equity firm headquartered in Paris, France. PAI
Partners invests in business services, food and consumer, general
industrials and healthcare.
In Australia, Audiotonix’s subsidiary Sound Devices LLC (Sound
Devices) is a wholesale supplier of portable and installed production
sound and video products for professional applications, including
wireless microphone systems.
The target, Soundtech Group, is a supplier of professional audio
micro-electronic mechanisms. Its subsidiaries include DPA
Microphones A/S (DPA Microphones), Austrian Audio GmbH
(Austrian Audio) and Wisycom S.r.l. (Wisycom).
In Australia, DPA Microphones and Austrian Audio are wholesale
suppliers of high-end microphones, and Wisycom is a wholesale
supplier of wireless microphone systems and wireless in-ear
monitoring systems.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
Audiotonix and Soundtech Group (together, the Parties) overlap in
the supply of wireless microphone systems in Australia. Soundtech
Group also supplies wireless in-ear monitoring systems in Australia.
Wireless microphone systems refer to audio solutions for use on
stage, on set or on the move that combine microphones, wireless
transmitters and receivers. Sound is recorded through a wireless
handheld microphone or through other types of microphones, such
as on-body or on-clothing microphones, that communicates with a
bodypack transmitter. The sound is transmitted to a wireless
receiver, which can be connected to various other
microphones/transmitters, and transfers the recorded sound to
other sound systems for processing. Antennas can be used as part
of the system to pick up radio frequency-transmitted sound over
longer distances.
Wireless in-ear monitoring systems refer to audio solutions used in
live, broadcast and theatrical environments and enable different
performers to hear all or relevant parts of the sound of the
performance. These systems combine wireless transmitters,
receivers worn on the body by the performers which transmit the
sound to an audio device, such as ear moulds, and combiners that
combine radio frequency outputs from several transmitters into a
single antenna.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form and publicly available information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• The Parties’ market share aggregation is estimated to be low on
any market definition, with an estimated post-acquisition market
share of below 10%.
• The Parties would continue to face competition from alternative
suppliers of wireless microphone and in-ear monitoring systems
in Australia, such as Sennheiser, Shure, Audio-Technica and
Lectrosonics.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

mergers.merger_description:
Medtronic plc (Medtronic), via its indirect wholly-owned subsidiary Project Saint Merger Sub, Inc., proposes to acquire Scientia Vascular, Inc. (Scientia) (the Acquisition).

Medtronic is a global healthcare technology company incorporated in Ireland and listed on the New York Stock Exchange. Globally (including in Australia), Medtronic develops, manufactures, distributes and sells device-based medical therapies and services within its cardiovascular, neuroscience and medical surgery portfolios. In Australia, Medtronic operates through its Australian subsidiary, Medtronic Australasia Pty Ltd, to supply medical devices, including neurovascular devices such as guidewires, catheters, aspiration catheters, embolic coils, flow diverters, liquid embolic agents, intrasaccular devices, AB stents and stent retrievers. In relation to the supply of guidewires in Australia, Medtronic supplies its Mirage .008” guidewire.

Scientia is a medical device company headquartered in West Valley City, Utah, USA. Scientia specialises in microfabrication technology for guidewires and catheters used in neurovascular and peripheral vascular procedures. In Australia, Scientia supplies guidewires under its “Aristotle” brand through a third-party distributor, LifeHealthcare Pty Ltd. The Aristotle-branded guidewires are supplied in Australia in four diameter configurations, .014”, .018”, .024”, and .035”.

Both Medtronic and Scientia supply neurovascular guidewires in Australia.

determination_table_content.details:
Medtronic plc (Medtronic), via its wholly owned subsidiary United
States Surgical Corporation, proposes to acquire Scientia Vascular,
Inc. (Scientia) (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Medtronic, is a global healthcare technology company
incorporated in Ireland and listed on the New York Stock Exchange.
Globally (including in Australia), Medtronic develops, manufactures,
distributes and sells device-based medical therapies and services
within its cardiovascular, neuroscience and medical surgery
portfolios.
In Australia, Medtronic operates through its Australian subsidiary,
Medtronic Australasia Pty Ltd, to supply medical devices, including
neurovascular devices such as guidewires, catheters, aspiration
catheters, embolic coils, flow diverters, liquid embolic agents,
intrasaccular devices, AB stents and stent retrievers. In relation to
the supply of guidewires in Australia, Medtronic supplies its Mirage
.008” guidewire.
The target, Scientia, is a medical device company headquartered in
West Valley City, Utah, USA. Scientia specialises in microfabrication
technology for guidewires and catheters used in neurovascular and
peripheral vascular procedures.
In Australia, Scientia supplies guidewires under its “Aristotle” brand
through a third-party distributor, LifeHealthcare Pty Ltd. The
Aristotle-branded guidewires are supplied in Australia in four
diameter configurations, .014”, .018”, .024”, and .035”.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
Medtronic and Scientia both supply neurovascular guidewires in
Australia.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form, information from third parties and publicly
available information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• For the supply of neurovascular guidewires in Australia, the
market share aggregation resulting from the Acquisition is
estimated to be low, and post-Acquisition, the merged entity will
continue to face competition from multiple alternative suppliers
of neurovascular guidewires.
• Some market participants were concerned that the Acquisition
could give rise to conglomerate effects by enabling Medtronic to
bundle its existing neurovascular medical devices with Scientia’s
guidewires, resulting in foreclosure of rival guidewire suppliers.
• The ACCC has had regard to:
1. the position of the Parties in their respective markets,
including Medtronic’s position as a supplier of a portfolio of
neurovascular medical devices,
2. the presence of alternative suppliers of both neurovascular
guidewires and other neurovascular medical devices, and
3. customer procurement processes for neurovascular medical
devices.
• The ACCC considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise
to conglomerate effects, as the merged entity would be unlikely
to have the ability and incentive to engage in various commercial
arrangements (such as offering bulk or multi-product discounts)
to the point of foreclosing rival neurovascular guidewire
suppliers and harming competition in the supply of
neurovascular guidewires.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

mergers.merger_description:
Australia and New Zealand Banking Group Limited, through its wholly owned subsidiary, ANZ NBH Pty Ltd (ANZ NBH) (together, ANZ) proposes to acquire Worldline SA (WL)’s interest in Worldline Australia Pty Ltd, trading as ANZ Worldline Payment Solutions (ANZWL), under a share sale and purchase agreement. ANZWL is currently a joint venture between ANZ and WL (the Parties), with ANZ holding 49% and WL holding 51%. Following the acquisition, ANZ will wholly own ANZWL.

ANZ is an ASX-listed financial services group. ANZ provides a range of banking products and services to retail and business customers in Australia and New Zealand, and to institutional customers globally. ANZ NBH is a non-operating intermediate holding company.

WL is a global merchant acquiring business, headquartered in France, and Europe’s largest merchant acquiring services provider. WL provides payment acceptance services and products to business customers internationally, including mobile authentication, digital banking, mobile payments and wallets, omni-commerce services, and account-to-account payments.

ANZWL was established in April 2022 when ANZ divested its merchant acquiring services business and referred business customers who required merchant acquiring services to ANZWL. The joint venture was intended to deliver enhanced payment solutions to ANZ’s merchant customers, while allowing ANZ to retain a significant ownership interest and an ongoing commercial relationship with the business.

The Parties overlap in the supply of payment solutions, including merchant acquiring, acceptance and value-added services, to business customers in Australia.

Henkel is a German public company listed on the Frankfurt Stock Exchange. In Australia, Henkel is active in the supply of hair care and hair styling products to hair professionals as well as to mass-market retailers, including its Schwarzkopf brand and Schwarzkopf sub brands, BC Bonacure, BLONDME, Chroma ID, Fibre Clinix, Fibreplex, got2b, Indola, Natural Styling, Oil Ultime, OSiS+, Palette, Session Label, Silhouette and TBH. Henkel also sells adhesive technologies in Australia.

Olaplex is a Delaware-registered company listed on the Nasdaq Global Select Market and headquartered in New York. In Australia, Olaplex primarily supplies premium hair products to online retailers and specialty retailers, as well as to hair professionals.

mergers.merger_description:
ServiceNow, Inc. (ServiceNow) proposes to acquire Veza Technologies, Inc. (Veza).

ServiceNow is a US software company. It supplies products which automate and simplify manual tasks to facilitate autonomous, coordinated workflows for organisations. These products can be applied across a range of operations including customer workflows (e.g. for order fulfilment), technology workflows (e.g. for IT and risk management and security operations), core business workflows (e.g. for HR employee onboarding) and creator workflows (workflows to support software developers). These services are delivered on ServiceNow’s ‘Now Platform’. ServiceNow also supplies IT security software to organisations via that platform.

Veza is a US security company that supplies Identity Access Management products, which are designed to enable companies to manage access to their data. These products are delivered via Veza’s ‘Access Platform’, which allows companies to monitor privilege access, investigate identity threats, automate access reviews, and introduce access governance to enterprise resources. Veza supplies identity governance and administration solutions, which are solutions that establish and manage the rules and processes governing access to systems. Veza has a very limited presence in Australia.

events.title: ServiceNow-Veza - Phase 1 Determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by a division of the Commission constituted by a direction issued pursuant to section 19 of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
ServiceNow, Inc. (ServiceNow)’s proposed acquisition of 100% of
the issued shares in Veza Technologies, Inc. (Veza) (the
Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

ServiceNow is a US software company. It supplies products which
automate and simplify manual tasks to facilitate autonomous,
coordinated workflows for organisations. These products can be
applied across a range of operations including customer workflows
(e.g. for order fulfilment), technology workflows (e.g. for IT and risk
management and security operations), core business workflows
(e.g. for HR employee onboarding) and creator workflows
(workflows to support software developers). These services are
delivered on ServiceNow’s ‘Now Platform’. ServiceNow also supplies
IT security software to organisations via that platform.
The target, Veza, is a US security company that supplies Identity
Access Management (IAM) products, which are designed to enable
companies to manage access to their data. These products are
delivered via Veza’s ‘Access Platform’, which allows companies to
monitor privilege access, investigate identity threats, automate
access reviews, and introduce access governance to enterprise
resources. Veza particularly focuses on identity governance and
administration (IGA) solutions, which are solutions that establish
and manage the rules and processes governing access to systems.
Veza has a very limited presence in Australia.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
The Parties both supply IT security software products:
• ServiceNow supplies IT security software products known
as security operations (SecOps) software and integrated risk
management (IRM) software. SecOps and IRM products
function as workflow management solutions and risk
trackers for customers.
• Veza supplies IAM software security products which govern
and monitor access to a customer’s IT systems.
While the Parties’ products can coexist within a customer’s
technology environment, they operate separately and perform
different functions for different purposes. As such, there is no
horizontal overlap between the Parties in Australia.
There are no vertical relationships between the Parties in Australia.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form as well as further information provided by
ServiceNow following pre-notification engagement with the ACCC.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market in
Australia. In reaching its decision, and based on the material before
it, the ACCC makes the following findings:
• Veza has a very limited presence in Australia.
• There are no horizontal overlaps or vertical relationships
between ServiceNow and Veza in Australia.
• The merged entity is unlikely to have the ability or incentive to
foreclose Veza’s competitors in IAM (including IGA) by
preventing their integration with ServiceNow, or to take other
actions that would lead to conglomerate competition concerns
in Australia. Also, ServiceNow’s offering relies on permitting
integrations with a wide range of different platforms, tools and
software in order to perform its functions and remain attractive
to customers.
• ServiceNow’s primary focus is the supply of integrated IT
workflow management software to enterprise customers. It
faces competition from alternative providers of such software,
including Microsoft, Broadcom, Oracle and Atlassian.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

mergers.merger_description:
North American Construction Group Limited (NACG), via NACG WA Acquisition Pty Ltd, proposes to acquire 100% of the shares in DCL Corp Pty Ltd, trading as Iron Mining Contracting, and Iron Hire Pty Ltd (the Targets) through a share purchase agreement.

NACG is a Canadian supplier of mining and heavy civil construction services to customers in the resource development, industrial construction and infrastructure sectors in Canada, the US, and Australia. Through its Australian subsidiaries, NACG supplies:

* heavy equipment rental services to mine operators with operators (wet hire) and without operators (dry hire) in New South Wales and Queensland;
* dry hire heavy equipment rentals in Western Australia;
* mining services to its heavy equipment rental customers in New South Wales and Queensland; and
* recycled and refurbished mining and construction equipment across Australia and worldwide.

The Targets supply mining services and civil construction services in Western Australia.

The parties both supply mining and civil construction services for large (Tier 1) and medium (Tier 2) scale mining projects.

events.title: North American Construction Group – Iron Mine Contracting and Iron Hire - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by a division of the Commission constituted by a direction issued pursuant to section 19 of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
The proposed acquisition by North American Construction Group
Limited (NACG), via NACG WA Acquisition Pty Ltd, of 100% of the
shares in DCL Corp Pty Ltd, trading as Iron Mining Contracting, and
Iron Hire Pty Ltd (together, the Targets) through a share purchase
agreement (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, NACG, is a Canadian supplier of mining and civil
construction services to customers in the resource development,
industrial construction and infrastructure sectors in Canada, the US,
and Australia. Through its Australian subsidiaries, NACG supplies:
• heavy equipment rental services to mine operators with
operators (wet hire) and without operators (dry hire) in New
South Wales and Queensland;
• dry hire heavy equipment rentals in Western Australia;
• mining and civil construction services to its heavy
equipment rental customers in New South Wales and
Queensland; and
• recycled and refurbished mining and construction
equipment across Australia and worldwide.
The Targets supply mining services and civil construction services in
Western Australia, as well as crushing and screening services.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
The Parties overlap in the supply of a range of mining and civil
construction services to large (Tier 1) and medium (Tier 2) mining
projects in Australia (Mining Services). Mining Services may involve
contract mining, civil earthmoving, project management, and
stockpile management.
NACG supplies Mining Services in Queensland and New South
Wales, only in conjunction with the supply of heavy equipment
rentals. The Targets supply Mining Services in Western Australia.
There are no current vertical relationships between the Parties,
however we understand that suppliers of Mining Services may rent
heavy equipment in the course of supplying Mining Services.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form, information provided by third parties and publicly
available sources.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings:
• while both Parties supply Mining Services, they supply these
services in different regions, and would likely continue to face
competition from significant alternative suppliers of Mining
Services;
• the Parties would likely continue to face competition from
significant alternative suppliers of heavy equipment rentals;
• the Parties would be unlikely to have an ability to engage in an
anti-competitive bundling or tying strategy, given the presence of
significant alternative suppliers of Mining Services and heavy
equipment rentals;
• the Parties would be unlikely to have an ability to foreclose rivals’
access to either Mining Services or heavy equipment rentals,
given the presence of significant alternative suppliers of both
Mining Services and heavy equipment rentals.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

mergers.merger_description:
The proposed acquisition involves a consortium comprised of Tega Industries Limited (**Tega**) and the Apollo funds indirectly and collectively acquiring all outstanding shares in the subsidiaries of AIP MC Holdings LLC that collectively form Molycop.

The Apollo Funds are managed by Apollo Global Management, Inc and indirect subsidiaries of it (**Apollo**).

The proposed acquisition will occur through a special purpose vehicle, Tega MC JV Holdings Pte. Ltd (**JV Co.**), which is indirectly and jointly controlled by Tega and the Apollo funds.  Tega indirectly holds 76.7% of JV Co., while Apollo indirectly hold the remaining 23.3%.

Tega is a global supplier of mining products such as mill and scrubber liners, trommels, screen media, hydrocyclones, slurry pumps and parts, conveyor components, chutes, and other rubber and composite wear parts. Tega also provides installation, condition monitoring, and maintenance services.

Apollo is an alternative asset management firm headquartered in New York. Examples of current investments include companies in natural resources, manufacturing and industrial, education, insurance, financial services, and leisure businesses.

Molycop is a global supplier of mining consumables, with a focus on the manufacture and sale of grinding media and flotation chemicals for use in both semi-autogenous grinding mills and ball mills. These products are used for mineral extraction and processing for copper, gold and iron ore. In Australia, Molycop also supplies engineered products for the Australian rail and manufacturing industries

events.title: Questionnaire - Tega/Apollo - Molycop

events.title: Tega and Apollo Funds consortium - Molycop - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by a division of the Commission constituted by a direction issued pursuant to section 19 of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
A consortium comprised of Tega Industries Limited (Tega) and funds
advised and/or managed by affiliates of certain subsidiaries of Apollo
Global Management, Inc (Apollo Funds) proposed acquisition of all
outstanding shares in the subsidiaries of AIP MC Holdings LLC that
collectively form Molycop (together, Molycop) (the Acquisition)]

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has determined
under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010
(Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer is a consortium comprising Tega and the Apollo Funds.
Tega is a supplier of mining products such as mill and scrubber liners,
trommels, screen media, hydrocyclones, slurry pumps and parts,
conveyor components, chutes, and other rubber and composite wear
parts. Tega also provides installation, condition monitoring, and
maintenance services.
Apollo is an alternative asset management firm headquartered in New
York. Examples of current investments include companies in natural
resources, manufacturing and industrial, education, insurance, financial
services, and leisure businesses.
The target, Molycop, is a global supplier of mining consumables, with a
focus on the manufacture and sale of grinding media and flotation
chemicals for use in both semi-autogenous grinding mills and ball mills.
These products are used for mineral extraction and processing of
copper, gold, and iron ore. In Australia, Molycop also supplies
engineered products for the Australian rail and manufacturing
industries.

Molycop and Tega supply complementary products. Molycop’s grinding
media and Tega’s mill liners are both inputs used by mining customers
in grinding mills to process ore. Grinding media are fed into grinding
mills together with ore. The rotation of the grinding mill creates impact
between the grinding media and ore, reducing the ore material down to a
finer size and liberating the minerals of value.
Key types of grinding media supplied by Molycop include:
• SAG balls: large, forged steel balls that operate with a semi-
autogenous (SAG) mill – the first stage of grinding ore after
crushing.
• Small / medium balls: smaller forged steel balls that operate a
ball mill – the second phase of grinding ore (after the SAG mill).
• High-chrome balls: typically smaller balls (also used in a ball
mill) with a higher chromium content, that have a lower wear
rate than forged steel but are more susceptible to breakage.
Mill liners, such as those supplied by Tega, are customised wear parts
that fit the interior grinding mills and ball mills to protect internal
surfaces from wear and impact during grinding to extend a mill’s life and
improve operational efficiency.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an acquisition to
be approved or subject to further assessment in Phase 2 in accordance
with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth)
(the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have regard to the object of the
Act and all relevant matters, including the interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering notified
acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines and interim
merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered the
information and documents that were submitted with the notification
form and information from third parties, and publicly available
information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into effect as
it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of
substantially lessening competition in any market. In reaching its
decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC makes the
following findings:
• There are no horizontal overlaps or material vertical relationships
between the parties.
• The ACCC has considered the potential for conglomerate effects,
particularly whether Tega would have the ability and incentive to
leverage Molycop’s strong market position in grinding media to
foreclose rival suppliers of mill liners by anti-competitively bundling
or tying the two products. However, the ACCC found:
− Many mining customers would be able to switch to a different
supplier of grinding media in response to any tying or bundling
strategy. While Molycop is the only supplier of Australian-made
grinding media and has a very high market share, they are
relatively commoditised products and customers are able to
switch to imported products.
− There would be practical barriers to bundling or tying the
products. For example, customers typically procure mill liners
and grinding media separately through different methods (spot
purchases for mill liners and competitive tenders for grinding
media) and over different time periods (grinding media are
typically supplied under longer term supply arrangements).

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by the
Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the Act,
applications for review of the determination are to be made to the
Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar days after
this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s Acquisitions
Register. To confirm whether there has been any application for review,
please contact the Australian Competition Tribunal.

mergers.merger_description:
Salesforce, Inc (**Salesforce**) proposes to acquire all of the outstanding voting securities of Qualified.com, Inc (**Qualified**).

Salesforce is a global supplier of customer relationship management (**CRM**) software and other software-as-a-service solutions that enable companies to manage and improve their relationships with customers. Salesforce’s core offerings are CRM solutions designed for the sales, service, marketing and commerce functions within a company. Salesforce, through Agentforce, also offers autonomous artificial intelligence (**AI**) agents that integrate across Salesforce’s services. Salesforce offers CRM and AI services to customers in Australia and abroad.

Qualified is a provider of the agentic marketing product “Piper”, which enables business-to-business (**B2B**) sales and marketing teams to improve customer prospecting and build a sales pipeline through AI-driven functionality. Piper is an inbound AI-based sales development representative (**SDR**) agent that was purpose-built to integrate with Salesforce’s CRM and uses Salesforce CRM data to deliver personalised experiences like custom greetings and targeted offers, while reporting customer engagement back into the Salesforce CRM system. Piper is Qualified’s only actively marketed product and is offered worldwide, including in Australia.

Both Salesforce and Qualified supply conversation automation solutions for B2B uses, which integrate with Salesforce’s CRM software. Conversation automation solutions for B2B uses includes tools, such as virtual assistants, AI agents, and chatbots, that support functions such as outbound and inbound sales prospecting for lead generation, customer qualification, customer research, meeting scheduling, and personalised product recommendations.

events.title: Salesforce - Qualified - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by a division of the Commission constituted by a direction issued pursuant to section 19 of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Salesforce, Inc’s (Salesforce)’s proposed acquisition of all of the
outstanding voting securities of Qualified.com, Inc (Qualified) (the
Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Salesforce, is a global supplier of customer
relationship management (CRM) software and other software-as-a-
service solutions that enable companies to manage and improve
their relationships with customers.
Salesforce, through Agentforce, offers a suite of artificial intelligence
(AI) agents, which are integrated with its core CRM offerings and
automate tasks related to sales, service, marketing and commerce
functions within a company. Offerings include personalised
engagement with potential and existing customers and scheduling
meetings. Salesforce offers CRM and AI services to customers in
Australia and abroad.
The target, Qualified, is a provider of an AI-based sales development
representative (SDR) agent, known as “Piper”, which enables
business-to-business (B2B) sales and marketing teams to improve
customer prospecting and build a sales pipeline through AI-driven
functionality. Piper was purpose-built to integrate with Salesforce’s
CRM software and uses Salesforce CRM data to deliver personalised
experiences like custom greetings and targeted offers, while
reporting customer engagement back into the Salesforce CRM
system. Piper is Qualified’s only actively marketed product and is
offered worldwide, including in Australia.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
Salesforce and Qualified (together, the Parties) overlap in Australia
in the supply of conversation automation solutions for B2B uses,
such as virtual assistants, AI agents and chatbots, to support sales
and marketing teams to engage with customers. Salesforce’s
Agentforce offering includes an outbound business development
representative, which is focused on generating new leads from those
that have not yet expressed interest in a company. Qualified’s Piper
is an inbound SDR focused on engaging prospective customers that
have already expressed interest in a company.
In addition to the horizontal overlap, there is a vertical relationship
between Qualified’s product Piper and Salesforce’s CRM platform.
Piper uses Salesforce’s CRM data to inform its agent’s interactions
and records information about customer engagement in
Salesforce’s CRM platform.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form and information from third partes.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• there is limited horizontal overlap between Salesforce and
Qualified in the supply of conversation automation services in
Australia, as the products the Parties supply target customers at
different points in the sales and marketing process. In addition,
Qualified has a small presence in Australia.
• the merged entity would continue to face competition from
alternative suppliers of conversation automation solutions, in
Australia and internationally.
• while there is a vertical relationship between the products
supplied by the Parties, the merged entity would be unlikely to
have the ability and incentive to foreclose rivals’ access to CRM
services given there are alternative suppliers of CRM services in
Australia and internationally. Salesforce may be able to offer
bundled or tied offerings to some customers, but we do not
consider this potential gives rise to conglomerate concerns.
Salesforce submits that a foundational characteristic of its
platform is it being open and interoperable, which limits
incentive it may have to foreclose rivals’ access.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

mergers.merger_description:
Banijay Group N.V., a listed company in Euronext Amsterdam, (together with the rest of the Banijay group, **Banijay Group**) and RedBird IMI (a joint venture between RedBird Capital Partners and IMI Media Group) propose to combine Banijay Group's content production and distribution business and part of its live event business and RedBird's All3Media into a newly incorporated company, Banijay Holding UK Limited (**CombinedCo**) (the **Acquisition**).

Post-Acquisition, Banijay Group will hold 50% of CombinedCo’s share capital and voting rights, and will have sole control of CombinedCo. Gold Rush TopCo Limited and an affiliate of funds advised by affiliates of RedBird together will hold the other 50% of CombinedCo’s share capital and voting rights.

Post-Acquisition, Banijay Group’s Banijay Entertainment business, and part of Banijay Live's business would form part of CombinedCo, along with All3Media.

Banijay Entertainment focuses on the production and distribution of audiovisual content with over 130 production companies.

In Australia, Banijay Entertainment is active through its subsidiary, Endemol Shine Australia. Endemol Shine Australia’s productions are broadcast across Australian free-to-air networks and their broadcast video on demand offerings, as well as subscription TV and subscription video on demand services. They include shows such as MasterChef, Australian Survivor, Big Brother, LEGO Masters, Married at First Sight, as well as dramas including NCIS: Sydney and Offspring. Banijay Live's business does not operate in Australia.

All3Media is a UK-based company active in the production and distribution of audiovisual content. All3Media’s current activities in Australia are:

* the distribution of content to broadcasters and streamers in Australia through All3Media International
* the production of one television program for Australian broadcaster Ten via its New Zealand subsidiary, South Pacific Pictures; and
* Little Dot Studios, a social media agency.

mergers.merger_description:
Volt Managed Investment Trust 1 and Volt Managed Investment Trust 2 (together, the **Volt MITs**), through their respective trustees, propose to invest A$355 million in HMC Capital Limited’s Energy Transition Platform (**HMC Platform**) through the acquisition of loan notes and share warrants issued by Volt HoldCo Pty Ltd (**Volt HoldCo**) (the **Acquisition**), with the warrants automatically converting into a minority equity interest in Volt HoldCo upon repayment of the loan notes. HMC Platform comprises wholly-owned subsidiaries of Volt HoldCo.

The Volt MITs are administered by professional trust companies, namely Perpetual Corporate Trust Limited, and The Trust Company (Australia) Limited, respectively (together, the **Perpetual Trustees**). The single unitholders in the Volt MITs are KKR Global Climate Transition Holdings Pte. Ltd and KKR Asia Climate Holdings Pte. Ltd respectively (the **KKR Funds**).

The Volt MITs are ultimately wholly owned by investment funds, vehicles and/or accounts advised and managed by various indirect subsidiaries of KKR & Co. Inc. (together with its direct and indirect subsidiaries, KKR).

KKR is a global investment firm that offers alternative asset management as well as capital markets and insurance solutions.

HMC Capital Limited (**HMC or HMC Capital**) is an ASX-listed alternative asset manager. The HMC Platform operates energy generation and storage assets and has a development pipeline of wind and energy storage projects.

KKR and the HMC Platform (the **Parties**) both operate within the electricity generation supply chain. The Parties horizontally overlap in the operation of electricity generating and storage assets in NEM. Some of HMC Platform’s assets and pipeline projects are, or will be, connected to transmission infrastructure in which KKR has an indirect and minority interest.

mergers.merger_description:
Trescal Holding Pty Ltd (**Trescal**), through a wholly-owned subsidiary, proposes to acquire TR Pty Ltd’s (**TR**) calibration and equipment sales business, TechRentals Calibration & Sales (**TR Calibration**) (the **Acquisition**).

Trescal supplies calibration services, equipment procurement and distribution, instrument verification, equipment maintenance, system validation, electrical safety equipment testing services and asset management services, to customers in various sectors. Trescal operates across Australia and has laboratories in Adelaide, Brisbane, Melbourne, Perth and Sydney. Trescal is a subsidiary of French company Trescal International SAS, a global provider of calibration and asset management services.

TR is primarily a nationwide provider of technology equipment (including testing, inspection, calibration and telecommunications equipment) for rent. Through the Acquisition, Trescal would acquire TR’s nationwide calibration services and equipment sales business, TR Calibration, which supplies calibration services and measurement equipment to customers across Australia including from laboratories in Sydney and Brisbane. TR is a subsidiary of Vp plc, a company listed on the London Stock Exchange.

Trescal and TR Calibration overlap in the supply of calibration services and calibration equipment in Australia. Calibration services involve comparing the performance of testing and measurement equipment against a measurement standard to ensure accurate performance and compliance with relevant standards, and related equipment repairs (for example, if equipment fails a calibration test).

ABL operates three subsidiaries in Australia:

* Transhipment Services Australia, which provides bulk cargo transhipment services utilising floating barges to transfer materials, such as iron ore, directly between smaller ships or shallow terminals and larger ocean-going vessels in deepwater berths.

mergers.merger_description:
General Dental Holdings Pty Ltd (trading as Impression Dental Group) (**IDG**) proposes to acquire 100% of the shares in Coastal Dental Care Australia Pty Ltd (**CDC**), through a share sale deed.

CDC is owned by Dr Franck Page and operates 15 dental practices across the Redland Bay and Gold Coast areas in Queensland and in Northern NSW under the Coastal Dental Care, Dental Implants & Aesthetics, and Gold Coast Holistic Dental Care names. CDC also has a dental laboratory (The Lab), a denture clinic (Gold Coast Dentures by Design) and a dental supplies organisation (Dental Plus).

IDG is an Australian-based dental support organisation that provides centralised services including recruitment, payroll, marketing, IT, finance, and operations, to enable dental practitioners to focus on providing dental services. IDG currently owns and supports 23 independently branded dental practices in Australia which provide dental services to the public, but IDG does not have any practices in the Redland Bay and Gold Coast areas or Northern NSW.

IDG is controlled by Genesis Capital, an Australian-based private equity firm that invests in businesses operating in the healthcare sector.

Genesis Capital’s investments also include Pacific Smiles Group (**PSG**) which sets up new dental centres from which dentists run their practice and provides operational support. PSG has over 120 dental centres across Australia, including 1 in the Redland Bay area, 5 on the Gold Coast, and 1 in Tweed. PSG’s dental centres are typically located inside shopping centres and operate under the Pacific Smiles Dental and nib Dental Care brands on the East Coast.

mergers.merger_description:
Sembcorp Industries Ltd (**Sembcorp**) proposes to acquire, via share sale agreement, 100% of Pioneer Sail Holdings Pty Limited and its subsidiaries including Alinta Energy Ltd (**Alinta Energy**) and Latrobe Valley Power (Holdings) Pty Ltd (**Latrobe Valley Power**), which operates the Loy Yang B coal-fired power station (together, the **Target Group**).

Sembcorp is an energy, renewable and industrial solutions company headquartered in Singapore with renewable energy and gas assets in the wider Asia region. Sembcorp does not own any operating assets in Australia, nor have any minority or controlling interests in any entities active in the energy sector in Australia.

Alinta Energy is an Australian "gentailer" which supplies electricity and gas to customers across Australia. It has over 3.4GW of installed and contracted generation assets comprising coal, gas, onshore wind and solar and a development pipeline of up to 10.4GW of renewable energy projects.

The Loy Yang B power station is a baseload brown coal fired power generator located 160km east of Melbourne which supplies electricity into the National Electricity Market. The Target Group also has joint venture interests in the Yandin Wind Farm and Spinifex Offshore Wind Project, which is currently in the planning and development phase.

Temasek is the largest shareholder of Sembcorp. Temasek has interests in a variety of industries globally, including financial services, telecommunications, transportation, and real estate and is headquartered in Singapore.

Temasek wholly-owns Singapore Power Group, an electricity and gas distribution company, which through its subsidiary Singapore Power International has an indirect interest in SGSP Australia Assets Pty Ltd (**SGSPAA**). SGSPAA owns Jemena, which is the owner and operator of gas distribution and transmission and electricity distribution network assets on the east coast of Australia and has interests in three electricity distribution networks located in New South Wales, Victoria and the Australian Capital Territory.

Temasek also has an interest in Neoen SA (**Neoen**), a France-headquartered global developer, constructor, financier, and operator of renewable energy assets. Neoen owns and/or operates a number of wind, solar and energy storage projects in New South Wales, Queensland, South Australia, Tasmania, and Western Australia.

determination_statement_of_reasons.text: When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines and interim merger process guidelines.

In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered the information and documents that were submitted with the notification form, additional information provided by the merger parties, market feedback and publicly available information.
For the reasons given below, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into effect.
Industry background
National Electricity Market

Electricity generated in eastern and southern Australia is traded through the National Electricity Market (NEM).1 The NEM consists of a wholesale spot market for selling electricity and a transmission grid for transporting it to energy customers.2 The market covers 5 regions – Queensland, New South Wales including the Australian Capital Territory, Victoria, South Australia and Tasmania, and services around 11 million customers.3

The NEM is operated by the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) through a centrally-coordinated dispatch process that pools generation from producers and delivers required quantities of electricity from the pool to wholesale consumers. This includes managing the bidding, scheduling and dispatch of generators, determining the spot price, measuring electricity use, and financially settling the market.4
Wholesale Electricity Market

Like the NEM, the Wholesale Electricity Market (WEM) is an electricity market that enables wholesale electricity sales between generators and retailers, large-scale customers and demand side participants. The market supplies energy to south-west Western Australia and operates on the South West Interconnected System (SWIS).5

The WEM has an additional capacity market, the Reserve Capacity Mechanism, which is unique to the WEM and aims to ensure there is sufficient generation capacity in the SWIS. 6 The Reserve Capacity Mechanism incentivises investment by awarding tradeable capacity credits to suppliers to guarantee capacity for the SWIS in a given year. 1 AER, State of the Energy Market 2025, 26 August 2025, p. 14. 2 AER, State of the Energy Market 2025, 26 August 2025, p. 16. 3 AER, State of the Energy Market 2025, 26 August 2025, p. 14. 4 AEMO, Dispatch information, accessed 30 April 2026. 5 AEMO, Energy markets and systems, accessed 30 April 2026. 6 AEMO, Energy markets and systems, accessed 30 April 2026.
Regulation of gas and electricity networks

Australia’s electricity and gas network assets are regulated under the National Electricity Law (NEL) and the National Electricity Rules (NER), and the National Gas Law (NGL) and National Gas Rules (NGR), respectively.
The ACCC considers the access and transparency and ring-fencing and non-disclosure obligations of these regulatory frameworks to be most relevant to its assessment of the Acquisition.
Transparency and access obligations
The NER and NGR set out a number of transparency and access obligations which apply to Jemena’s electricity distribution networks and gas transmission and distribution pipelines, respectively.

Chapter 3 of the NER includes requirements for the provision of information by, and to, AEMO, including information about existing and proposed generation projects, network congestion, available capacity of generators and networks, adequacy of electricity supply, the operation of the spot market, and requirements for ancillary services.7 In addition, information regarding proposed and approved revenue and pricing by Distribution Network Service Providers (DNSPs) is made available through the Australian Energy Regulator’s (AER) public draft and final determinations pursuant to Chapter 6 of the NER.8

Chapter 5 of the NER provides frameworks for establishing and modifying connection and access to electricity networks by generators, including through published standard offers for connection and processes for negotiated connections and dispute resolution.9

Part 10 of the NGR prescribes information that gas pipeline service providers must make publicly available on their websites, which includes demand, capacity and pricing information.10 Access to this information is designed to assist users in negotiations with pipeline service providers.

Gas pipelines owned by Jemena are subject to a negotiate-arbitrate model under the NGL and NGR. This framework is designed to promote negotiated access to gas pipeline services, with the ability for access disputes to be arbitrated. Access disputes can include price and non-price matters.11
Ring-fencing and non-disclosure obligations

Ring-fencing refers to separating the regulated activities of an electricity DNSP or gas pipeline operator from contestable parts of their business. These requirements include restrictions on sharing information obtained through regulated electricity or gas 7 Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC), Chapter 3 of the NER. 8 AEMC, Chapter 6 of the NER. 9 AEMC, Chapter 5 of the NER. 10 AEMC, Part 10 of the NGR. 11 AER, Pipeline Access Dispute Guide, November 2023. operations with related businesses that operate in contestable markets, for example in retailing electricity or gas.

The AER’s Electricity Distribution Ring-fencing Guideline, made under the NER, imposes obligations designed to prevent cross-subsidisation and discriminatory conduct between an electricity DNSP’s regulated network services and its activities in contestable markets. 12

Ring-fencing obligations apply to non-public information which a DNSP acquires or generates in connection with its regulated operations, including information concerning individual customers or classes of customers. A DNSP is generally:

items: required to protect the confidentiality of that information, and only use the information for the purpose for which it was acquired or generated. This obligation is subject to certain exceptions including consent, complying with legal requirements or where required for the DNSP or requesting party to provide services
prohibited from disclosing information to a related business where it is obtained from a current or potential competitor and would likely advantage the related business, and
required to provide access to such information to competitors of a related business on request where it has disclosed such information to a related business.13

determination_statement_of_reasons.text: Rule 8.6 of the NER sets out obligations for registered participants in the NEM (including DNSPs) regarding confidential or commercially sensitive information. These provisions generally require participants to use all reasonable endeavours to keep confidential any confidential information that comes into its possession or control, or of which it becomes aware. It contains several exceptions, including consent, complying with legal requirements and disclosure in connection with arbitration or legal proceedings.14

Gas distribution networks are also subject to ring-fencing obligations under the NGL. These ring-fencing provisions require the separation of pipeline businesses from related businesses, such as those that produce, buy or sell gas.

Additionally, Part 16 of the NGR sets out obligations relating to the protection of confidential information against improper use or disclosure, subject to limited exceptions including consent, complying with legal requirements and disclosure in connection with arbitration or legal proceedings. These obligations require gas pipeline operators to take all practical steps to protect confidential information from improper disclosure or use and to, upon request, disclose specific information to the relevant person to whom the information relates (or a person acting on their behalf).15 12 AER, Ring-fencing Guideline Electricity Distribution Version 4, 27 February 2025. 13 AER, Ring-fencing Guideline Electricity Distribution Version 4, 27 February 2025, clauses 4.1 and 4.3. 14 AEMC, Rule 8.6 of the NER. 15 AEMC, Part 16 of the NGR.
Overlap and relationship between the parties

Sembcorp itself does not own any operating assets in Australia or have any minority or controlling interests in any entities active in the energy sector in Australia. However, Temasek, the largest shareholder of Sembcorp, has indirect minority interests in both Jemena and Neoen, which give rise to vertical links and horizontal overlaps respectively. Temasek and its related entities are referred to in these reasons as
Temasek Group.
Relationships between Jemena and the Target Group

Temasek is an indirect, minority shareholder of Jemena through its wholly owned subsidiary, Singapore Power Limited (SP Group). Singapore Power International Pte Ltd (SPI), a subsidiary of SP Group, has a 40% interest in Jemena’s parent company, SGSPAA, with the remaining equity held by State Grid International Development Australia Investment Company Ltd (State Grid) as SGSPAA’s controlling shareholder.

Jemena is an owner and operator of gas transmission and distribution and electricity distribution network assets on the east coast of Australia. Jemena owns or otherwise has interests in three electricity distribution networks, located in the ACT and Victoria, and two gas distribution networks, located in the ACT and NSW. Jemena also owns gas transmission pipelines on the east coast of Australia (listed in Table 1 below).

Jemena’s gas and electricity assets which have a relationship to Alinta Energy’s operations are detailed in Table 1 below. There are no other vertical relationships between Jemena and other entities in the Target Group. Table 1 Jemena assets and relationship with Alinta Energy Asset type State Relationship with Alinta Energy Gas transmission Eastern Gas Pipeline NSW/VIC Alinta Energy’s Bairnsdale Power Station connects to the Eastern Gas Pipeline. There are no other gas-fired power stations connected to the Eastern Gas Pipeline in Victoria. A number of competing gas-fired power stations also connect to the Eastern Gas Pipeline in NSW including Energy Australia's 760MW Tallawarra power station. VicHub Interconnect Facility VIC Alinta Energy utilises the VicHub Interconnect to transport gas from the Victorian Declared Wholesale Gas Market to customers along the Eastern Gas Pipeline or in the Sydney Short Term Trading Market. Gas distribution Jemena Gas Distribution NSW Alinta Energy utilises Jemena's gas distribution Network network to deliver gas to its retail customers in NSW. Information from the parties indicates that Alinta Energy has a less than 10% share of the retail supply of gas customers to each of C&I and small-use customers in NSW. Electricity distribution Jemena Electricity Distribution VIC Alinta Energy utilises the Jemena and United Network Energy electricity distribution networks to deliver electricity to its retail customers in Victoria. VIC Information from the parties indicates that Alinta United Energy Distribution Energy has a less than 10% share of the retail Network (34% share) supply of gas customers to each of C&I and small-use customers in Victoria.
As outlined in Table 1 above, Jemena’s infrastructure assets overlap vertically with Alinta Energy’s:

items: electricity generation operations in Victoria
retail supply of gas in New South Wales, and
retail supply of electricity in Victoria.

determination_statement_of_reasons.text: Horizontal overlap with Neoen

Temasek has an indirect 14.19% interest in Neoen. Neoen is a France-headquartered global developer, constructor, financier and operator of renewable energy assets. Within Australia, Neoen owns and/or operates a number of wind, solar and energy storage projects in New South Wales, Queensland, South Australia, Tasmania and Western Australia.

Alinta Energy and Neoen overlap in the generation and supply of wholesale electricity to each of the NEM and WEM, as shown in Table 2. There are no other overlaps between Neoen and other entities in the Target Group. Table 2 Horizontal overlaps between Neoen and Alinta Energy State Alinta Energy asset Neoen generation asset Queensland Braemar Power Station (564 MW) Western Downs Green Power Hub (460 MW) Collinsville Solar Farm (PPA) (29 MW) Western Downs Battery Stage 1 (270 MW) Rugby Run Solar Farm (PPA) (52 MW) Western Downs Battery Stage 2 (270 MW) Kaban Green Power Hub (157 MW) New South Wellington North Solar Farm (PPA) (32 Dubbo Solar Farm (28 MW) Wales MW) Parkes Solar Farm (66 MW) Griffith Solar Farm (36 MW) Western Pinjarra Cogeneration Plant (285 MW) Collie Battery Stage 1 (219 MW) Australia Wagerup Power Station (412 MW) Collie Battery Stage 2 (341 MW) Yandin Wind Farm (214.2 MW) Badgingarra Wind and Solar Farm (PPA) (150 MW) Walkaway Wind Farm (PPA) (89 MW)

There is no current overlap between Alinta Energy and Neoen in South Australia. However, Alinta Energy does have some projects in development, including the Reeves Plain Energy Hub (300 MW), and Neoen has existing facilities including the Goyder South Wind Farm (412 MW) and Blyth Battery (238.5 MW).

Neoen and Alinta Energy also both supply storage and firming capacity to the WEM. Alinta Energy does not currently have any storage and firming projects in the NEM but has several projects in development. Similarly, both parties participate in markets for the supply of essential system services and frequency control services and electricity derivatives and contracting markets.
Competition assessment

The ACCC has considered the effects of the Acquisition by comparing the likely future state of competition if the Acquisition proceeds against the continuation of the current state of competition (being the likely future state of competition if the Acquisition does not proceed).

Operators of monopoly electricity network assets and gas pipelines tend to have substantial market power. Regulation and market design, including in the operation of the NEM and WEM, seeks to constrain the exercise of that market power. Despite this, vertical integration involving monopoly energy infrastructure assets has the potential to raise material competition concerns that require close consideration. Operators of monopoly energy infrastructure assets may leverage their market power into vertically related markets to foreclose competition through discrimination against rivals, preferential treatment of affiliates or sharing competitively significant information.
Vertical effects – foreclosure of rival electricity generators in Victoria and the NEM

The ACCC considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of substantially lessening competition in relation to the generation and wholesale supply of electricity in Victoria and the NEM. The ACCC considered the supply of storage and firming capacity, essential system support services and frequency control services, and derivatives within the broader assessment of wholesale electricity rivalry in the circumstances of this matter.

The ACCC has considered the ability and incentive of the merged entity to hinder, delay or otherwise frustrate rival electricity generators in a way that would substantially lessen competition, through its ownership links with Jemena. Cross-ownership between Alinta Energy’s market energy operations and Jemena’s monopoly infrastructure electricity network assets and gas transmission pipeline assets, has the potential to raise vertical foreclosure risks that need to be closely considered.

The ACCC has had regard to the level of control and influence that Temasek Group has over Jemena in order to assess the likely vertical effects of the Acquisition. Based on information and documents provided by the merger parties and third parties, the ACCC accepts that Temasek Group is unlikely to be able to positively direct Jemena’s decision making or operations. However, the ACCC considers that Temasek Group is able to influence certain investment decisions by SGSPAA.

In coming to this view, the ACCC has had regard to the size of Temasek Group’s shareholding in SGSPAA, the rights, interests and incentives of State Grid, the composition of the SGSPAA board, how critical operational decisions are made by Jemena, and the governance arrangements and conflicts policies that exist within Jemena.

The ACCC’s assessment of vertical effects in electricity generation has focused on the potential for Temasek Group to foreclose the Target Group’s electricity generation rivals by hindering or preventing Jemena’s investment in its gas/electricity network and gas transmission assets as required to facilitate access or expansion by generation rivals.
Based on information from the merger parties, market feedback and publicly available information, the ACCC considers that:

items: There are no committed or anticipated material generation projects which require further investment in Jemena’s gas or electricity assets that the ACCC is currently aware of.

Rival electricity generators may be able to access sufficient alternatives to Jemena’s gas and electricity assets, which would limit the ability and incentive to foreclose. For example, gas-fired generation rivals may be able to access inputs through alternative gas pipelines or receipt points, and most generators connect to electricity transmission networks (where Jemena does not have any interests) rather than electricity distribution networks.

A strategy to foreclose a material rival generator may not be profitable for Temasek Group. Post-acquisition, Temasek Group will hold similar partial interests in Jemena and the Target Group (via Sembcorp). A foreclosure strategy could result in a loss of profits for Temasek Group through Jemena not supplying services, or supplying a lower volume of services, to the rival generator (unless the Target Group connects a generator to Jemena’s network at the expense of the foreclosed rival). The Target Group would likely only recapture a very small proportion of those lost profits downstream given: − its less than 10% share of registered capacity in Victoria as well as across the NEM, and − stymieing a specific rival generator is likely to create market conditions that may support a different generator entering the market, which is not reliant on connecting to Jemena’s network assets or gas supply from Jemena’s gas transmission assets.

The regulatory frameworks in electricity and gas markets (described at paragraphs 2.9–2.21 above), may mitigate the likelihood of Temasek Group utilising its minority shareholding in Jemena to prevent or hinder network investment.

determination_statement_of_reasons.text: Having regard to the combination of factors identified in paragraph 2.38 above, the ACCC considers that Temasek Group would be unlikely to have the ability and/or incentive post-Acquisition to foreclose rival electricity generators through its ownership links with Jemena.
Vertical effects – foreclosure of Alinta Energy’s rivals in the retail supply of gas in New South Wales and retail supply of electricity in Victoria

In the ACCC’s view, the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of substantially lessening competition in relation to the retail supply of gas in New South Wales and the retail supply of electricity in Victoria because Alinta Energy would be unlikely to have the ability and/or incentive post-Acquisition to harm the competitiveness of rivals in a way that substantially lessens competition through its access to and use of competitively significant information.

In this case, the ACCC focused on the potential for Alinta Energy, as part of the merged entity, to access and use competitively significant information held by Jemena to undermine the ability and incentive of rival gas and electricity retailers to compete effectively. For example, information about rival retailers’ customers or innovative business customer/household energy solutions that require the involvement of the network business.

The ACCC considers that information would generally need to be quite specific, down to the level of individual or small groups of customers, in order for Alinta Energy to gain competitive advantages. Higher-level information about market shares and strategies is unlikely to be competitively significant, as such information is commonly available in the market.

Alinta Energy’s access to competitively significant information held by Jemena would likely be contrary to Jemena’s regulatory obligations under Part 16 of the NGR, Rule 8.6 of the NER and AER Ring Fencing Guidelines (see paragraphs 2.17-2.21 above).

These regulatory requirements may, to some extent, mitigate Alinta Energy’s ability to access and use competitively significant information held by Jemena. However, the ACCC also considers that there is a degree of circumvention risk where a party has an incentive to engage in conduct which is contrary to regulatory requirements.
Nonetheless, the ACCC considers that foreclosure strategies by Alinta Energy which utilise competitively significant information are unlikely because:

items: Temasek Group is unlikely to have sufficient positive control over Jemena (as identified at paragraph 2.35 above) to combine Alinta Energy’s access to competitively significant information with preferential network treatment from Jemena

any use of competitively significant information in the absence of preferential network treatment would likely only provide transient advantages to Alinta Energy in retail supply as competitors respond to Alinta Energy’s observed commercial strategies and/or become aware of the information because of transparency and access requirements that apply to Jemena (described at paragraphs 2.11-2.15 above), and
transient benefits from the use of competitively significant information may not offset the regulatory risks involved with the disclosure and use of that information.

determination_statement_of_reasons.text: Reduced competition arising from horizontal aggregation in the generation and supply of wholesale electricity to each of the NEM and the WEM

The ACCC considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to substantially lessen competition in the generation and supply of wholesale electricity to the NEM and/or WEM as a result of the removal of competition between Alinta Energy and Neoen. The ACCC considered the supply of storage and firming capacity, essential system support services and frequency control services, and derivatives within the broader assessment of wholesale electricity rivalry in the circumstances of this matter.
The ACCC has had regard to the level of control and influence that Temasek Group has over Neoen as part of its assessment of the potential for reduced competition post- Acquisition.

In the ACCC’s view, Temasek Group is unlikely to be able to exercise positive control or material influence over Neoen’s operations. In coming to this view, the ACCC has had regard to information provided by the merger parties about the size of Temasek Group’s indirect shareholding (at 14.19%) relative to other shareholders, arrangements for the appointment of directors on the Neoen board, how critical operational decisions are made by Neoen, and governance arrangements within Neoen.
Although Temasek Group will hold interests in both Alinta Energy and Neoen, based on the material before it, the ACCC considers that:

items: Temasek’s minor interest in Neoen makes it unlikely that Alinta Energy and Neoen will compete as a single entity in the generation and supply of wholesale electricity, and their incentives to compete are unlikely to materially change as a result of the Acquisition.

Even if Alinta Energy and Neoen competed as a single entity, it would have a relatively small market share. Based on information from the merger parties: − Alinta Energy and Neoen would have a combined share of 10% of registered capacity in the Queensland region of the NEM and less than 5% in the New South Wales region of the NEM. They do not overlap in other NEM states. Across the NEM, Alinta Energy and Neoen would have a combined, <10% share of total registered capacity or dispatchable electricity capacity in the NEM. − Although the combined Alinta Energy/Neoen would have a higher share of registered capacity in the WEM (20-30%), as highlighted in market feedback, the ACCC notes that these figures may overstate the significance of the overlap as they do not reflect differences in generation portfolios or in load factor and intermittency.

Similarly, Alinta Energy and Neoen will continue to face competition from alternative suppliers of wholesale electricity. Competitors in the NEM include AGL Energy, Origin Energy, Stanwell and Energy Australia. In the WEM, alternative generators include Synergy and NewGen and BlueWaters (which are both owned by Sumitomo Corporation).

Abbott Laboratories (**Abbott**), via its wholly owned subsidiary Badger Merger Sub I, Inc, proposes to acquired 100% of the shares in Exact Sciences Corporation (**Exact Sciences**) through an Agreement and Plan of Merger.

Abbott is a global healthcare company, headquartered in the US, active in the provision of diagnostics, medical devices, nutrition, and branded generic pharmaceuticals.

Exact Sciences is a US headquartered company active in the provision of advanced molecular diagnostics, specialising in cancer screening, precision oncology diagnostics, and genetic testing.

The parties both supply oncological diagnostic products in Australia.
